subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> be three statements such that the truth value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> . Then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> be three statements such that the truth value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> . Then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively :
solution:
Given that the truth value of p∧q→~q∨r is F .

For the above truth value to be possible, p∧q should be TRUE and ~q∨r should be FALSE.

p∧q is TRUE when p & q both are TRUE.

~q∨r is FALSE when q is TRUE and r is FALSE.

So, the truth values of p, q, r are T, T, F respectively.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Among the two statements

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q is a contradiction and S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q is a tautology
question:
Among the two statements

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q is a contradiction and S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q is a tautology
solution:
Given,

S1:p⇒q∧p∧~q

≡~p∨q∧p∧~q as A⇒B≡~A∨B

≡~p∧p∧~q∨q∧p∧~q

≡f∧~q∨f∧p

≡f∨f≡f

 

S2:p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q

≡p∧q∨~p∧q∨p∧~q∨~p∧~q

≡p∨~p∧q∨p∨~p∧~q

≡t∧q∨t∧~q≡q∨~q≡t

Hence, both statement are true.

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet". Select the correct negation from the following:
question: Consider the statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet". Select the correct negation from the following:
options: The match will not be played and weather is not good and ground is wet.
If the match will not be played, then either weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will not be played or weather is good and ground is not wet.
solution:
Let p: weather is good
and q: ground is not wet

Given statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet" can be written as p∧q

Now, negation of  p∧q is ~p∧q≡~p∨~q

which is equivalent to "The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet".

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is NOT equivalent to:
question: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is NOT equivalent to:
solution:
Given,

p⇒q∨p⇒r

Now using the formula A⇒B≡~A∨B

We get, ~p∨q∨~p∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

≡p⇒q∨r, so option C is true.

Now solving option 1 we get,

 p∧~r⇒q

≡~p∧~r∨q

≡~p∨r∨q

≡~p∨r∨q

≡~p∨r∨~p∨q

≡p⇒q∨p⇒r, so option 1 is also true,

Now solving option 4 we get, 

p∧~q⇒r

≡~p∧~q∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

≡~p∨q∨~p∨r

≡p⇒q∨p⇒r, so option 4 is also correct.

Hence option B is only wrong.

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to
question: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)=\sim p \vee(q \rightarrow p) \\
& =\sim p \vee(\sim q \vee p) \quad \text { since } p \vee \sim p \text { is always true } \\
& =\sim p \vee p \vee q=p \rightarrow(p \vee q) .
\end{aligned}
$$

q: The contrapositive of the statement “If you are born in India, then you are a citizen of India”, is
question: The contrapositive of the statement “If you are born in India, then you are a citizen of India”, is
options: If you are not born in India, then you are not a citizen of India.
If you are a citizen of India, then you are born in India.
If you are born in India, then you are not a citizen of India.
If you are not a citizen of India, then you are not born in India.
solution:
As we know, contrapositive of p→q is ∼q→∼p
Hence, required contrapositive statement is “If you are not a citizen of India, then you are not born in India”.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $p$ be the statement " $x$ is an irrational number", $q$ be the statement " $y$ is a transcendental number", and $r$ be the statement " $\mathrm{x}$ is a rational number iff $\mathrm{y}$ is a transcendental number".
Statement $-1$ : $r$ is equivalent to either $q$ or $p$
Statement $-2: r$ is equivalent to $\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)$.
question:
Let $p$ be the statement " $x$ is an irrational number", $q$ be the statement " $y$ is a transcendental number", and $r$ be the statement " $\mathrm{x}$ is a rational number iff $\mathrm{y}$ is a transcendental number".
Statement $-1$ : $r$ is equivalent to either $q$ or $p$
Statement $-2: r$ is equivalent to $\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)$.
options: Statement $-1$ is false, Statement $-2$ is true
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true, Statement $-2$ is a correct explanation for Statement $-1$
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true; Statement $-2$ is not a correct explanation for Statement $-1$.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is false.
solution:
Given statement $r=\sim p \leftrightarrow q$
Statement $-1: r_1=(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$
Statement $-2: r_2=\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)=(p \wedge q) \vee(\sim q \wedge \sim p)$
From the truth table of $r, r_1$ and $r_2$, $r=r_1$.
Hence Statement $-1$ is true and Statement $-2$ is false.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following three statements:

A If 3+3=7 then 4+3=8

B If 5+3=8 then earth is flat.

C If both A and B are true then 5+6=17.

Then, which of the following statements is correct?
question:
Consider the following three statements:

A If 3+3=7 then 4+3=8

B If 5+3=8 then earth is flat.

C If both A and B are true then 5+6=17.

Then, which of the following statements is correct?
solution:
Truth table for if-then statement is

p q p→q
T T T
T F F
F T T
F F T

A 3+3=7 (false), and 4+3=8 (false), therefore A is true.

B 5+3=8 (true) and earth is flat (false), hence B is false.

C Since, B is false and 5+6=17 (false), hence C is true.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>∇</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> be such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∇</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∆</mo><mi>r</mi></math> is logically equivalent to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>∇</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced></math> be such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∇</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∆</mo><mi>r</mi></math> is logically equivalent to
solution:
Case-I

If Δ≡∇≡∧

p∧q→p∧q∧r

then it is false if r is false.

So not a tautology

Case-II

If Δ≡∇≡∨

p∨q→p∨q∨r≡ tautology

then p∨q∨r≡pΔr∨q

Case-III

If Δ=∨,∇=∧

then p∧q→p∨q∧r is not a tautology

when p→T,q→T,r→F

Case-IV

If Δ=∧,∇=∨

p∧q→p∧q∨r is not a tautology

q:

S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r and S2:p→q∨r
Then, which of the following is NOT true?
question:

S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r and S2:p→q∨r
Then, which of the following is NOT true?
solution:
S1:~p∨q∨~p∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

S2:p→q∨r≡~p∨q∨r

So S1 is equivalent to S2

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two statements, then which of the following compound statement is a tautology?
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two statements, then which of the following compound statement is a tautology?
solution:
LHS of all the options are same i.e. ((P→Q)∧~Q)

≡(~P∨Q)∧~Q

≡(~P∧~Q)∨(Q∧~Q)

≡~P∧~Q

(A) (~P∧~Q)→Q

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨Q

≡(P∨Q)∨Q≠ tautology

(B) (~P∧~Q)→~P

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨~P

≡(P∨Q)∨~P

(C) (~P∧~Q)→P

≡(P∨Q)∨P≠ Tautology

(D) (~P∧~Q)→(P∧Q)

≡(P∨Q)∨(P∧Q)≠ Tautology

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following statement is a tautology?
question: Which of the following statement is a tautology?
solution:
From De Morgan's Law,

~p∨q=~p∧~q

p→q=~p∧~q

~p∧q→p∨q

=~~p∧q∧~p∧~q

=~~p∧f

=~f=t, where f is contradiction and t is tautology.

subject: Mathematics
q: The Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to :
question: The Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to :
solution:
(p⇒q)∧(q⇒~p)

=(~p∨q)∧(~q∨~p)  {p⇒q=~p∨q}

=(~p∨q)∧(~p∨~q)  {commutative property}

=~p∨(q∧~q)   {distributive property}

=~p

≡~p∨q∧~q∨q

≡~p∨q∧t≡~p∨q

Now, 

p∧q⇒~p∨q

solution:

Hence $\mathrm{P} \wedge \mathrm{Q} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$, false

The contrapositive statement of this statement is:
question: Consider the statement: "For an integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math>, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is even, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is odd". The contrapositive statement of this statement is:
options: For an integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math>, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is even, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is odd.

For an integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math>, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is not even, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is not odd.

For an integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math>, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is even, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is even.

For an integer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math>, if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is odd, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is even.
solution:
P:n3-1  is even, q:n is odd

contrapositive of  p→q=~q→~p

⇒ "if n is not odd then n3-1  is not even"

⇒For an integer n if n is even, then n3-1 is odd.

q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">~</mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo mathvariant="italic">∨</mo><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is not equivalent to :
question: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo mathvariant="italic">~</mo><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo mathvariant="italic">∨</mo><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is not equivalent to :
solution:
Given statement is B⇒~A∨B

Truth table for given statement:

A B ~A ~A∨B B⇒~A∨B
T T

F

 T T
T F F F T
F T T T T
F F T T T

It is a tautology.

Truth table of given options:

A⇒B ~A⇒B B⇒A⇒B A⇒~A⇒B B⇒~A⇒B
T T T T T
F T T T T
T T T T T
T F T T T

Option A: B⇒A⇒B is a tautology.

Option C: A⇒~A⇒B is a tautology.

Option D: B⇒~A⇒B is a tautology.

Hence, it is very clear that given statement is not equivalent to option B.

solution:
We know the conditional statement:

if A, then B is given by A→B

So the given statements If I have fever, then I will take medicine and I will take rest can be written as,

P→~Q∧R

q: The negation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
question: The negation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>)</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
solution:
The given statement is p∧~q∨~p

Let us take A∩Bc∪Ac.

Now let us apply distributive law to the above statement.

≡A∪Ac∩Bc∪Ac

≡U∩Bc∪Ac

≡Bc∪Ac

Now the given statement p∧~q∨~p will be equivalent to ~q∨~p or ~p∨~q.

Hence the given statement is equivalent to ~p∨~q,

Now negation of given statement by demorgan's law will be,

~~p∨~q≡p∧q

solution:
p ∧ ∼q ∨q∨∼p∧q

Set equivalent 

=A ∩B- ∪A- ∩B ∪B

=(A ∪B-A∩B)∪B

=A ∪B

Hence answer is p ∨q .

subject: Mathematics
q:
Among the statements:

S1: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r

S2:p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r
question:
Among the statements:

S1: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r

S2:p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r
solution:
We have,

S1: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r

For S1:  If p is F, q is T, r is F , then

p∨q⇒r≡F

p⇒r≡T

So, p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r≡F

then F⇒F≡F

i.e. S1 is not a tautology

S2: p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r

S2:  If p is T, q is F, r is F , then

p∨q⇒r≡F

p⇒r∨q⇒r≡T

then p∨q⇒r⇔p⇒r∨q⇒r≡F

i.e. S2 is not a tautology

q: Negation of the statement: <math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is an integer or <math><mn>5</mn></math> is irrational is:
question: Negation of the statement: <math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is an integer or <math><mn>5</mn></math> is irrational is:
options: <math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is not an integer <math><mn>5</mn></math> is not irrational
<math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is not an integer and <math><mn>5</mn></math> is not irrational
<math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is irrational or <math><mn>5</mn></math> is an integer
solution: Using<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></math>, we get negation of given statement as <math><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> is not an integer and <math><mn>5</mn></math> is not irrational Number.
